# ABSTRACT {.unnumbered}

::: {style="text-align: center"}
**DESIGN OF A CHEATING DETECTION SYSTEM FOR THE GZCTF PLATFORM USING TWO-STAGE SIMILARITY ANALYSIS AND WEIGHTED RISK SCORING ALGORITHMS**

Dimas Maulana
:::

Capture The Flag (CTF) competitions have become a widely adopted method for cybersecurity
education, yet their integrity is threatened by cheating practices such as flag sharing and
illegal collaboration between teams. The GZCTF platform only provides reactive log monitoring,
leaving cheat detection dependent on manual inspection that is inefficient and prone to human
error. This research aims to design and build a cheating detection system embedded as middleware
within the GZCTF ASP.NET Core architecture. The system is developed using the Waterfall model
with a Two-Stage Analysis approach: the first stage records request metadata synchronously, while
the second stage analyzes it asynchronously. Collusion detection is performed through Sequence
Similarity Analysis, combining the Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) and Jaccard Similarity into a
Relative Sequence Index (RSI), validated by a Confidence Screening Detector (CSD) to form collusion
groups. All anomaly indicators are quantified through Weighted Risk Scoring (WRS) into a cumulative
risk score per team. The system is evaluated using functional (black box) testing, accuracy testing
based on a confusion matrix, performance testing, and usability testing with the System Usability
Scale (SUS). The testing results show [hasil pengujian], targeting recall of at least 0.90, precision
of at least 0.85, and a false positive rate of at most 0.10. The developed system is expected to
improve monitoring efficiency and preserve academic integrity in CTF competitions.

**Keywords:** GZCTF, cheat detection, Sequence Similarity Analysis, Weighted Risk Scoring, academic integrity

{{< pagebreak >}}
